content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 23, 1993


Barbara Booth Grunwald
Deputy County Counsel
County of Fresno
2220 Tulare Street
Fifth Floor
P.O. Box 1549
Fresno, CA 93716

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-93-465

Dear Ms. Grunwald:

This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of your advice to a county employee who has an outside counseling business regarding his responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your situation does not involve a governmental decision, we can only provide this general explanation of the requirements of the Act.² Please note that our reply only relates to the provisions of the Act. If you have questions regarding other provisions of law outside of our jurisdiction, such as Government Code section 1090, we recommend that you contact the Attorney General's Office.

QUESTION

An employee of the County of Fresno who serves as a Drug Services Manager for the Substance Abuse Division has asked you whether his outside employment with Pacifica Counseling Associates ("Pacifica") creates a conflict of interest.

___________________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-93-465
Page 2

CONCLUSION

The Political Reform Act does not prohibit a public official
from simultaneously being employed privately. Instead, the Act
prohibits public officials from making, participating in making or
using their positions to influence a governmental decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
their financial interests.

FACTS

The County of Fresno has an employee who serves in the
position of Drug Services Manager for the Substance Abuse
Division. This employee also holds outside employment with
Pacifica Counseling Associates, where the employee does private
counseling. The County of Fresno contracts out for similar
counseling services with various private sector agencies. The
employee has advised you that Pacifica is not one of these
contractors.

DISCUSSION

The Act does not prohibit a public official from being
employed outside of government. Instead, the Act prohibits any
public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section
87100.)

Pursuant to Section 87100, the income received by the
County's Drug Services Manager in his or her capacity as a private
counselor would be an economic interest in that source of income,
and thus the employee would be prohibited in his or her official
capacity from participating in any decision on behalf of the
County of Fresno that would have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on that source of income. (Section 87103.)

Therefore, so long as the employee disqualifies himself or
herself from these types of decisions, he or she will have no
conflict of interest under the Act with regard to his or her
employment with Pacifica Counseling Associates.

For future reference, we have enclosed a pamphlet entitled
"California's Conflict of Interest Laws for Public Officials"
which summarizes the Act's conflict of interest laws.

Our File No. I-93-465
Page 3


If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.3

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant

SGC:ld

Enclosures




3  Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
